para.text: Finning Prohibition.
“Finning” means removing only the fins of a spiny dogfish and returning the remainder of the dogfish to the sea.
Finning is prohibited.
Spiny dogfish shall be landed with the tail and fins attached naturally to the corresponding carcass.
An individual may remove the head and eviscerate a spiny dogfish at sea, as long as the tail and fins remain attached by a portion of uncut skin.
Commercial Licenses and Permits.
A person shall be licensed in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-701, Annotated Code of Maryland, in order to catch, possess, or land spiny dogfish for commercial purposes.

A vessel which is used to catch, possess, or land spiny dogfish for commercial purposes from the waters of the federal Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) of the Atlantic Ocean, which are defined as those ocean waters between 3 and 200 miles offshore, shall be permitted by the National Marine Fisheries Service in accordance with 50 CFR §648.4.
Commercial Season and Catch Limits.
The annual commercial quota for Maryland is established by the National Marine Fisheries Service and the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission.
The season for catching spiny dogfish is May 1 through April 30.
The commercial catch limits for spiny dogfish shall be established and may be modified through a public notice issued in accordance with §J of this regulation.
The public notice establishing the commercial catch limit issued in accordance with §J of this regulation shall state the catch limit in pounds per vessel per day for an individual:
Licensed to catch finfish;
Licensed to catch finfish and in possession of a Maryland striped bass permit that has been registered in the Atlantic Ocean fishery; and
Licensed to catch finfish and in possession of a Maryland spiny dogfish landing permit.

An individual may not land spiny dogfish (Squalus acanthias) from waters of the EEZ for commercial purposes when the Regional Administrator for National Marine Fisheries Service determines that the EEZ quota has been attained and closes the fishery as permitted in accordance with 50 CFR 648.
Regardless of the number of authorized individuals on board any one vessel, no more than one spiny dogfish vessel limit may be landed from one vessel per day.
Spiny Dogfish Landing Permit Declaration.

In order to obtain a spiny dogfish landing permit, a tidal fish licensee shall declare their intent to fish for spiny dogfish by paying the permit fee in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-701, Annotated Code of Maryland, between August 1 and March 31 of the following year, dates inclusive.

A tidal fish licensee who has not declared by March 31 may apply to the Department, provided the licensee has not declared late in either of the 2 preceding years and shows good reason why the application should be processed, until April 14 of the current year, or the next business day if April 14 occurs on a weekend.

An exception to the deadlines in §D(2) of this regulation will be considered only for an individual who can provide satisfactory documentation of a physical or mental incapacity that prevented that individual from meeting the declaration time period.
An individual who fails to declare may no longer declare in subsequent years unless the individual receives a permit through §E(7) or §G of this regulation.
An individual is eligible to declare for a permit to catch and land spiny dogfish in Maryland if they:
Are licensed in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-701, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Meet the vessel requirements described in §D(6) of this regulation; and
Meet the following requirements:

The individual provides proof that they owned or had a share of ownership in a vessel that landed at least 1,000 pounds of spiny dogfish between May 1, 2004, and March 8, 2013, and have not previously held a Maryland spiny dogfish landing permit;

The individual holds a spiny dogfish landing permit that is valid for the current season and owns a vessel that landed a minimum of 15,000 pounds of spiny dogfish within 3 seasons of obtaining that permit; or
The individual has received a spiny dogfish landing permit in accordance with §E(7) of this regulation or through a transfer in accordance with §G of this regulation within the past 3 seasons.
Vessel Requirements.
An individual shall own or have a share of ownership in a vessel to be eligible to declare for a spiny dogfish landing permit.
If there is more than one owner of a vessel, a licensee may declare the vessel on the spiny dogfish landing permit if the licensee:
Owns at least 51 percent of the vessel; or
Has permission from the other owners who, whether individually or in combination, own at least 51 percent of the vessel.
Only one owner of a vessel may declare the vessel on a spiny dogfish landing permit.

The federally registered name or the State registration numbers of the vessel owned by the permittee shall be indicated at the time of application for the permit and declared on the Maryland spiny dogfish landing permit.
Any change in vessel ownership shall be reported to the Department.
The fee for a spiny dogfish landing permit is $25.
If an individual is no longer eligible to declare for a spiny dogfish landing permit, the permit shall be deemed relinquished to the Department.
Spiny Dogfish Landing Permit.
A spiny dogfish landing permit is valid from May 1 through April 30.
An individual may not be issued more than two spiny dogfish landing permits regardless of the number of qualifying vessels the individual owns or in which the individual has a share of ownership.

Proof of eligibility for a Maryland spiny dogfish landing permit as required in §D(5) of this regulation shall be documented by records of the Department or records of the National Marine Fisheries Service.

A permittee may catch, possess, or land spiny dogfish for commercial purposes on a vessel other than the vessel declared on the permittee's permit only if in possession of the permit issued to the permittee.
Temporary transfers of spiny dogfish landing permits are not permitted.
Spiny dogfish landing permits that have been relinquished or revoked may be issued to another individual in accordance with §E(7) of this regulation.
Distribution of Spiny Dogfish Landing Permits that have been Relinquished or Revoked.
If a spiny dogfish landing permit is relinquished or revoked, the Department shall publish a notice on the Fisheries Service website announcing that applications for permits are being accepted.
The public notice shall state the application process.
The Secretary shall make a reasonable effort to disseminate public notice through various other media so that an affected person has reasonable opportunity to be informed.
Operators.
Definition.
In this regulation, the following term has the meaning indicated.
Term Defined. “Operator” means an individual who:
Is licensed to catch or land fish for commercial purposes in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-701, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Is not a permittee; and
Acts as an agent of a permittee.
The name of the vessel on which the operator is working shall be declared on the Maryland spiny dogfish landing permit.
A permittee may allow one operator to use the permittee’s spiny dogfish landing permit if the permittee identifies the operator to the Department.
A permittee may change the identified operator once each permit year.
When engaged in permitted activities, an operator shall be:
In possession of the permittee’s spiny dogfish landing permit; and
On the vessel declared on the spiny dogfish landing permit.
Permanent Transfer of a Landing Permit.
A commercial tidal fish licensee may permanently transfer a spiny dogfish landing permit if:
The transferor:
Applies to the Department for the transfer on forms provided by the Department;
Held the permit for 4 or more consecutive years; and
Harvested a minimum of 20,000 pounds within the previous 4-year period on the same vessel that qualified for the permit under §D(5) of this regulation; and
The transferee:
Is a valid commercial tidal fish licensee; and
Has paid the applicable fees required by Natural Resources Article, §4-701, Annotated Code of Maryland.
An authorized representative of a deceased licensee may permanently transfer a spiny dogfish landing permit:
Regardless of the number of years the deceased licensee held the spiny dogfish landing permit;
Regardless of the amount of spiny dogfish the deceased licensee landed; and
Without transferring the deceased’s tidal fish license.
Reporting and Penalties.
An individual shall record the harvest of spiny dogfish in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-206, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A dealer shall transmit information weekly, or as required, on each spiny dogfish transaction through the Department-approved reporting system.
In addition to any other penalty, failure to comply with this regulation may result in the suspension of the current permit or the denial of a subsequent permit.

Prior to suspending a permit or denying an application for a permit under this regulation, the Department shall give the licensee notice of its intended action and an opportunity to appear at a hearing conducted in accordance with the contested case procedures set forth in State Government Article, Title 10, Subtitle 2, Annotated Code of Maryland, and COMAR 08.01.04.
Commercial Gear.
Definition.
In this regulation, the following term has the meaning indicated.
Term Defined. “Roundfish gill net” means a gill net that is constructed with floats on the float line and that has no tie-down twine between the float line and the lead line.
In waters of the Atlantic Ocean, during the period from November 1 through March 31, a roundfish gill net with mesh size equal to or greater than 5.25 inches and less than 10 inches:
May not be set prior to 5 a.m. each day; and
Shall be removed from the water no later than 8 p.m. each day.
The restriction in §I(2) of this regulation applies only to a commercial tidal fish licensee who:
Holds a valid Maryland spiny dogfish permit; and
Does not hold a federal spiny dogfish permit.
General

The Secretary may establish or modify catch limits or seasons for spiny dogfish in order to implement the Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission Interstate Fishery Management Plan for Spiny Dogfish, by issuing a public notice on the Department’s website.
The public notice shall state its effective hour and date and shall be published on the Department’s website at least 48 hours in advance of the effective hour and date.
The Secretary shall make a reasonable effort to disseminate public notice through various other media so that an affected person has reasonable opportunity to be informed.
The Secretary shall make a reasonable effort to modify catch limits to ensure that the quota is harvested and not exceeded.
A violation of the restrictions set by the Secretary in accordance with §I of this regulation is a violation of this regulation.
Spiny dogfish harvested for commercial purposes from Maryland waters of the Atlantic Ocean or from the waters of the EEZ and landed in Maryland shall be sold to a dealer with a federal permit.
Spiny dogfish harvested for commercial purposes shall only be harvested from a vessel.

section.heading: Violations of Business Regulation Article, §9A-501.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.

para.text: The Board may seek regulatory action against an individual who violates Business Regulation Article, §9A-501.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Such adjudication shall proceed in manner set forth in Business Regulation Article, §9A-311, Annotated Code of Maryland.

annotations.annotation: Business Regulation Article, §§9A-205(a)(1), and 9A-302(e) and (f), 9A-310(a)(1)(xii), and 9A-401(c), Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: June 19, 1995 (22:12 Md. R. 900)

para.text: A licensee, permit holder, or registrant shall be familiar with and comply with the laws and regulations governing the practice of beauty culture.
Every licensee and registrant shall:
Display a current and valid license in full view of the public at the practitioner's work station;
Affix a current picture to the license; and
Have available at all times valid government-issued photographic identification.
When a senior cosmetologist or cosmetologist practices cosmetology in the residence of a patron, in a nursing home, or in a hospital, the following conditions shall be met:
The services are sponsored by an authorized salon;

The licensee is fully supplied with sanitized and disinfected equipment by the sponsoring salon and the implements and tools are carried back and forth in an appropriate manner to assure they are not contaminated;
The sponsoring salon maintains complete records of all services performed, and the date, time, place, and charge for the services;
All tools, equipment, and complete records are available for inspection;
Board inspectors are permitted to conduct inspections of any area where cosmetology services are provided; and
When the cosmetology services are provided in a nursing home, hospital, or similar facility, the:
Patron is a:
Resident of the facility, and
Customer of the beauty salon, preexisting or otherwise;
Facility does not hold itself out to the public as a provider of cosmetology services; and
Compensation paid for the cosmetology services is ultimately paid to the licensee performing the services or to the sponsoring beauty salon.
A licensee operating a barber shop shall comply with all pertinent sanitation regulations contained in COMAR 09.22.02.04—.06.
A photography studio that provides hair styling or makeup services as a part of a photo package shall obtain an owner's permit, and the services shall be performed by an authorized licensee.
A licensee, permit holder, or registrant shall promptly notify the Board of any change of address.
The Board may initiate a criminal prosecution of a person who engages in the practice of beauty culture without a valid license or registration.
A licensee or registrant is responsible for the sanitary condition of the licensee's or registrant's individual work station.
A licensee, permit holder, or registrant may not knowingly interfere with the lawful activity of an inspector of the Board.
The provision of cosmetology, esthetic, or nail technology services in a beauty salon is prima facie evidence that compensation has been, or will be, paid for the services rendered.
An esthetician license authorizes the licensee to engage in providing esthetic services as defined in Business Occupations and Professions Article, §5-101(o), Annotated Code of Maryland.

The term “massaging”, as used in Business Occupation and Professions Article, §5-101(o), Annotated Code of Maryland, means contact with the skin limited to the beautification, cleansing, and embellishment of the skin during the application and removal of skin care products.
The term “exfoliating” as used in Business Occupation and Professions Article, §5-101(o), Annotated Code of Maryland, means to remove dead skin cells, dirt, bacteria, and excess oil from the skin.

The term “cosmetic microneedling” as used in Business Occupation and Professions Article, §5-101(o), Annotated Code of Maryland, means the use of cosmetic, non-medical devices that have needles no longer than 1 mm and are used in a manner that does not penetrate the skin beyond the epidermis.

The term “toning” as used in Business Occupation and Professions Article, §5-101(o), Annotated Code of Maryland, means the cleaning, manipulating and stimulating of skin by use of facials, face masks, and wraps.

An eyelash extension technician license authorizes the licensee to engage in the practice of applying, removing, cleansing, and maintenance of semi-permanent individual or volume eyelash extensions to enhance the length, curl, fullness, and thickness of natural lashes.
Temporary Eyelash Extension Technician License.
The Board may issue a temporary eyelash extension technician license to an applicant who submits an application on or before December 31, 2026.
An applicant for a temporary license shall submit to the Board an application on the form that the Board provides.
To qualify for a temporary license, an applicant shall be at least 17 years old and have completed successfully a ninth grade education or the equivalent.
Applicants for a temporary license are exempt from the training and exam requirements of Business Occupations and Professions Article, §5-305(c) and (h), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The term of a temporary license is 1 year.
The fee for a temporary license is $11.
The Board may issue no more than one temporary license to an individual.
Renewal.
Temporary licenses may be renewed no more than one time for a term of 1 year.
An applicant for the renewal of a temporary license shall submit to the Board an application on the form that the Board provides.
The Board shall not renew a temporary license unless the applicant demonstrates good cause as to why they did not obtain a permanent eyelash technician license and that they are
Enrolled in an eyelash extension course approved by the State Department of Education or the Maryland Higher Education Commission; or
Registered as an apprentice as provided under Business Occupations and Professions Article, §§5-404 and 5-505, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The fee for the renewal of a temporary license is $11.
A cosmetologist apprentice registration authorizes the apprentice to learn all aspects of cosmetology under the direct supervision of a senior cosmetologist.
One senior cosmetologist shall directly train one cosmetologist apprentice.
Before becoming eligible to take the cosmetologist examination, a cosmetologist apprentice shall:
Train at least 20 hours per week;
Train for not less than 24, nor more than 36, months; and
Complete the apprenticeship curriculum approved by the Board.
For good cause, the Board may issue a new cosmetologist apprentice registration to an individual previously registered as a cosmetologist apprentice if:
A minimum of 6 months has passed since the individual was last registered as a cosmetologist apprentice; and
The individual surrenders the number of hours earned under any other cosmetologist apprentice registration.
A cosmetologist apprentice registration shall be obtained, and used, only for the purpose of receiving the amount of training required to qualify for a licensing examination given by the Board.

An esthetician apprentice registration authorizes the esthetician apprentice to learn all aspects of providing esthetic services as defined in Business Occupations and Professions Article, §5-101(l), Annotated Code of Maryland.

The term “massaging”, as used in Business Occupation and Professions Article, §5-101(l), Annotated Code of Maryland, means contact with the skin limited to the beautification, cleansing, and embellishment of the skin during the application and removal of skin care products.
One senior cosmetologist or one licensed esthetician with 2 years experience shall directly train one esthetician apprentice.
Before becoming eligible to take the esthetician examination, an esthetician apprentice shall:
Train at least 20 hours per week;
Train for not less than 12 or more than 24 months; and
Complete the apprenticeship curriculum approved by the Board.
For good cause, the Board may issue a new esthetician apprentice registration to an individual previously registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or an esthetician apprentice if:
A minimum of 6 months has passed since the individual was last registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or an esthetician apprentice; and
The individual surrenders the number of hours earned under any other cosmetologist apprentice or esthetician apprentice registration.
An esthetician apprentice registration shall be obtained, and used, only for the purpose of receiving the amount of training required to qualify for a licensing examination given by the Board.
An esthetician apprentice registration may be renewed one time.
A nail technician apprentice registration authorizes the nail technician apprentice to learn all aspects of manicuring and pedicuring.
One senior cosmetologist or one licensed nail technician with 2 years experience shall directly train one apprentice.
Before becoming eligible to take the nail technician examination, a nail technician apprentice shall:
Train at least 20 hours per week;
Train for not less than 8 or more than 12 months; and
Complete the apprenticeship curriculum approved by the Board.
For good cause, the Board may issue a new nail technician apprentice registration to an individual previously registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or a nail technician apprentice if:
A minimum of 6 months has passed since the individual was last registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or a nail technician apprentice; and
The individual surrenders the number of hours earned under any other cosmetologist apprentice or nail technician apprentice registration.
A nail technician apprentice registration shall be obtained, and used, only for the purpose of receiving the amount of training required to qualify for a licensing examination given by the Board.

An eyelash extension technician apprentice registration authorizes the eyelash extension technician apprentice to learn all aspects of applying, removing, cleansing, and maintaining semi-permanent individual or volume eyelash extensions to enhance the length, curl, fullness, and thickness of natural lashes.

Beginning January 1, 2027, one senior cosmetologist who has completed a Board approved continuing education course regarding eyelash extension services or one licensed eyelash extension technician with 2 years’ experience as a licensed practitioner shall directly train one apprentice.
Before becoming eligible to take the eyelash extension technician examination, an eyelash extension technician apprentice shall:
Train at least 10 hours per week;
Train for not less than 3 or more than 7 months; and
Complete the apprenticeship curriculum approved by the Board.

For good cause, the Board may issue a new eyelash extension technician apprentice registration to an individual previously registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or an eyelash extension technician apprentice if:
A minimum of 6 months has passed since the individual was last registered as a cosmetologist apprentice or an eyelash extension technician apprentice; and
The individual surrenders the number of hours earned under any other cosmetologist apprentice or eyelash extension technician apprentice registration.

An eyelash extension technician apprentice registration shall be obtained and used only for the purpose of receiving the amount of training required to qualify for a licensing examination given by the Board.
Prior to supervising an apprentice for the first time, a licensee shall participate in an orientation program administered by the Board.
A licensee who supervises an apprentice shall:
Ensure that the apprentice receives:
At least 20 hours of training per week; and
The required theory and practical training;
File a monthly report, on a form supplied by the Board, stating the progress of each apprentice in the salon and identifying the designated licensee in charge of each apprentice;
Advise the Board immediately, in writing, when an apprenticeship is discontinued, temporarily or otherwise; and
Ensure that any apprentice is limited to and performs only the services authorized by the apprentice registration.

Unless the Board determines that exceptional circumstances exist, if a monthly report required by §A(2) of this regulation is not received by the Board within 90 days of the completion of the apprentice training:
The report may not be received by the Board; and
Credit may not be given for the training.

section.heading: Apprenticeship Registration Requirements.
section.text: A person may be registered as an apprentice if the person:

para.text: Is at least 16 years old;
Has secured a sponsoring salon in which to apprentice;
Has been approved by the Board; and
Prior to being registered as an apprentice, has participated in an orientation program administered by the Board.
The Board shall schedule examinations as often as necessary to meet the demand.
Each qualified applicant for examination shall be sent a notice of the examination that contains the:
Time, date, and location of the examination;
Amount of time allotted for the examination;
Areas that will be tested during the theoretical and practical portions of the examination;
Requirements for models;
Equipment the applicant shall bring;
Requirements for passing the examination; and
Board's policy on bringing books or other materials into the examination room.
Requirements for Taking Examination.
A candidate shall arrive in a timely fashion to take the examination.
A candidate shall present the notice of examination and a proper identification with the candidate's signature and photograph.
A candidate may not bring books or papers into the examination room and may not use those materials during either the practical or the theoretical portion of the examination.
A candidate taking the practical portion of an examination shall:
Appear in a clean, washable garment;
If taking the cosmetologist examination, appear with an appropriate mannequin head;
If taking the nail technician examination, appear with an appropriate mannequin hand;
If taking the esthetician examination:
Be accompanied by a live model who is 16 years old or older; or
Appear with a mannequin head; and
Appear with the equipment necessary to take the examination which shall be in a clean and disinfected condition and in good repair.
Disqualification. A candidate who violates any of the provisions of §C of this regulation may not be admitted to the examination and shall forfeit the examination fee.
Interpreters—Foreign Language Dictionaries.
The Board may permit a Board-approved interpreter to accompany an applicant and assist in the theory portion of the examination.
If approved by the Board or its authorized representative, an applicant may use a word-for-word, bilingual dictionary during the theory portion of an examination.
During any portion of an examination:
A sign interpreter may assist an applicant who is hearing impaired; and
A reader may assist an applicant who has dyslexia or another, similar reading impairment.
When a practical test cannot be completed for any reason, the grade for that test shall be “incomplete” and not “failure.”
Passing Examination.
In order to be certified as passing an examination, a candidate shall:
Receive a score of 75 percent or more on the practical portion of the examination;
Score at least 75 percent or more on the theoretical portion of the examination; and

Demonstrate a sufficient ability to verbally communicate and comprehend the English language in order to understand, converse, and properly respond to the requests, needs, and explanations of English speaking patrons.
An individual who passes one or more portions of the examination shall be excused from retaking the portion passed.
An applicant who has not passed all parts of the examination within 1 year of the original examination date shall be required to retake the entire examination.
Content of Cosmetologist Examination.
A cosmetologist examination shall consist of a theoretical portion and a practical portion.

The theoretical portion of the cosmetologist examination shall consist of questions which embrace the subjects of cosmetology found in basic cosmetology textbooks including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and disinfection;
Chemical applications;
Hair cutting and styling techniques;
Diseases and disorders;
Facial massage;
Skin care;
Makeup application;
Hair removal;
Application of artificial nails;
Manicuring and pedicuring procedures; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of cosmetology.
The practical portion of the cosmetologist examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Wet set or thermal curl set;
Permanent cold wave;
Chemical relaxing;
Hair pressing;
Croquignole curl;
Bleach and tint;
Blended haircut;
Fingerwave;
Pincurls;
Roller placement; and
Manicure.
Content of Senior Cosmetologist Examination.
A senior cosmetologist examination shall consist of a theoretical portion only.
A candidate shall be required to demonstrate a knowledge of cosmetology including, but not limited to, an advanced knowledge of:
Sanitation and disinfection techniques;
Chemical applications;
Hair cutting and styling techniques;
Diseases and disorders; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of cosmetology.
Content of Esthetician Examination.
An esthetician examination shall consist of a theoretical portion and a practical portion.

The theoretical portion of the esthetician examination shall consist of questions which embrace the subjects of esthetics found in basic esthetics textbooks including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and disinfection;
Diseases and disorders;
Facial massage;
Skin care;
Makeup application;
Hair removal; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of esthetics.
The practical portion of the esthetician examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Patron preparation;
Skin cleansing;
Skin analysis;
Location and names of motor points;
Tweezing and arching of eyebrows;
Massage movements; and
Application of women's daytime makeup.
Contents of Nail Technician Examination.
A nail technician examination shall consist of a theoretical portion and a practical portion.

The theoretical portion of the nail technician examination shall consist of questions which embrace the subjects of manicuring found in basic manicuring textbooks including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and disinfection;
Diseases and disorders;
Manicuring and pedicuring procedures;
Application of artificial nails; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of a nail technician.
The practical portion of the nail technician examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Basic manicure;
Hairline tip of a free edge;
Half moon;
Application of acrylic nails; and
Pedicure procedure.
Contents of Blow Drying Examination.
A blow drying examination shall consist of a theoretical portion and a practical portion.

The theoretical portion of the blow drying examination shall consist of questions which embrace the subjects of beautifying, cleaning, and arranging hair found in cosmetology textbooks including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and disinfection;
Disease and disorders;
Arranging hair;
Cleansing hair;
Curling hair;
Dressing hair;
Blow drying hair;
Singeing hair; and
Beautifying hair.
The practical portion of the blow drying examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Arranging hair;
Cleansing hair;
Curling hair;
Dressing hair;
Blow drying hair;
Singeing hair; and
Beautifying hair.
Content of Hair Stylist Examination.
A hair stylist examination shall consist of a theoretical and a practical portion.

The Theoretical portion of the hair stylist examination shall consist of questions which embrace the subjects of the hair styling found in basic cosmetology textbooks, including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and disinfection;
Chemical applications;
Hair cutting and styling techniques;
Diseases and disorders; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of hair styling.
The practical portion of the hair styling examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Wet set or thermal curl set;
Permanent cold wave;
Chemical relaxing;
Hair pressing;
Croquignole curl;
Bleach and tint;
Blended haircut;
Fingerwave;
Pincurls; and
Roller placement.
Content of Eyelash Extension Examination.
An eyelash extension examination shall consist of a theoretical and a practical portion.
The theoretical portion of the eyelash extension examination shall consist of questions that relate to the subject of eyelash extensions, including, but not limited to, the following:
Sanitation and safety;
Lash extension types, adhesives, and tools;
Lash anatomy and growth cycles;
Contraindications and allergic reactions; and
Laws and regulations regarding the practice of cosmetology.
The practical portion of the eyelash extension examination shall consist of the candidate demonstrating and explaining:
Lash isolation;
Lash extension application;
Adhesive control;
Lash extension removal;
Client safety measures; and
Sanitation practices.

Purpose. This regulation is intended to carry out the provisions of Business Occupations and Professions Article, Titles 4 and 5, and Business Regulation Article, §§2-106.13 and 2-106.14, Annotated Code of Maryland. In particular, it is intended to set various fees in order to cover the actual documented direct and indirect costs of fulfilling the statutory and regulatory duties of the State Board of Barbers and State Board of Cosmetologists.
Definitions.
In this regulation, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Barbers Board” means the State Board of Barbers.
“Cosmetologists Board” means the State Board of Cosmetologists.
“License fee” means the fee paid by an applicant or licensee, as applicable, in connection with the issuance and renewal of a license and the issuance of a limited license and a reciprocal license.
“Salon owner permit fee” means the fee paid by an applicant or licensee, as applicable, in connection with the issuance and renewal of a salon permit.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Labor.

Examination Fees. An applicant for examination or reexamination shall pay an examination or reexamination fee directly to the testing service chosen by the Cosmetologists Board to develop or administer the examination.

Average of Costs. In order to establish fees that more equitably distribute the costs associated with the operation of each board among similar boards, the Secretary may average the direct and indirect costs of the Barbers Board and the Cosmetologists Board, provided that the boards consent to having their direct and indirect costs averaged together.
As provided for in Business Regulation Article, §§2-106.13 and 2-106.14, Annotated Code of Maryland, and this regulation, the Cosmetologist Board sets the following license and permit fees:
Salon owner — $56;
Senior cosmetologist — $28;
Cosmetologist — $28;
Blow dry — $28;
Esthetician — $28;
Nail technician — $28;
Hair stylist — $28;
Eyelash extension technician — $28;
Apprentice — $11;
Reinstatement:

If the license of a senior cosmetologist, cosmetologist, esthetician, or nail technician license has expired, a reinstatement fee of $28 shall be paid in addition to the renewal fee in this section; and
If the permit of a salon owner has expired, in addition to the renewal fee in this section:
A reinstatement fee of $56 shall be paid if not more than 45 days have passed since the expiration of the permit; and
A pre-opening inspection fee of $169 shall be paid, and such an inspection shall be conducted, if more than 45 days have passed since the expiration of the permit; and
Pre-opening inspection — $169.
A separate payment is required for each application.
Payment in cash may not be accepted.
All fees are nonrefundable.
A renewal application for each license, permit, or registration shall be mailed to each licensee, permit holder, or cosmetologist apprentice at the last known address.
A licensee, permit holder, or cosmetologist apprentice shall submit each application and the required payment at least 14 days before the expiration date of the existing license.
An application that is incomplete or is not accompanied by the appropriate fee shall be returned to the applicant.
A registered cosmetologist apprentice is limited to two renewals.
A limited practice apprentice registration is not renewable.
Prior to the expiration of each 2-year term, a licensee shall provide proof of satisfactory completion of continuing education requirements.
Examiners, appointed by the Board, shall be paid a per diem of at least $50 based on the State's budget.
Inspectors, appointed by the Board, shall be paid a per diem of at least $50 based on the State's budget.
All per diem employees are entitled to be reimbursed for traveling expenses in accordance with the State's policy.

section.heading: Citations to Licensees.
section.text: Unless otherwise indicated, the following civil penalties are applicable to the following violations of the laws and regulations of the Board of Cosmetologists discovered by a Board inspector during an inspection of a beauty salon:

para.text: Working in a beauty salon for which a permit never has been issued or other unauthorized location — $300;
Licensing:
Operating without a valid license — $300;
No photo on license — $100;
No valid government-issued photo identification — $100;
Services beyond scope — $300;
Sanitation:
Improper disinfection:
Failure to wash implements before disinfection — $150;
Failure to properly disinfect implements, bowls, basins, other areas — $150;
Failure to properly store disinfected implements — $150;
Failure to keep clean disinfectant on esthetician tray or nail technician table — $150;
Improper procedures for cut or blood-related incidents — formal hearing;
Other:
Poor lighting, heating, or ventilation — $50;
Unclean walls, curtains, or floor coverings — $50;
Absence of hot or cold running water — $50;
Presence of rodents or insects — $50;
Use of salon (shop) for sleeping or living — $100;
Failure to promptly remove hair or debris — $50;
Unsanitary restroom — $50;
No soap dispenser and disposable towels — $50;
Unclean operator's garments — $50;
Failure to wash hands before each client — $50;
No wet sanitizer at station — $50;
No closed drawer or cabinet for disinfected implements at station — $100;
Use of neck duster, powder puff, sponge, septic pencil, or lump alum on more than one client — $100;
Improper application, removal, or discarding of lotions, oils, and other liquids — $100;
Improper application of creams — $100;
Improper maintenance of lotions, oils, and other liquids — $100;
Failure to properly cover head, foot rest, or manicuring cushion — $100;
Improper storage or disposal of towels — $50;
Improper sanitation of pressing combs or curling irons — $100;
Insufficient number of combs or brushes — $50;
Failure to use protective neck strip — $50;
Improperly washed or sanitized shampoo bowl — $50;
Improper cleansing or sanitation of retention rods — $100;
Failure to remove soiled items from top of work station after use — $50;
Placement of pins or clips in mouth — $50;
Use of implements dropped on floor without cleaning and disinfecting — $100;
Failure to sharpen lip or eyebrow pencil after each use — $100;
Failure to discard disposable implement or cosmetic after use — $100;
Use of hair removal wax for more than one client — $300;
Failure to launder nondisposable draping after each client — $50;
Unsanitary manicuring or eyelash extension table or surrounding area — $100;
Improper paraffin wax procedures — $100;
Improper use of electric files, bits, or sanding bands — $100;
Improper procedures when working on acrylic nails — $100;
Improper procedures when performing eyelash extension services — $100;
Failure to label eyelash adhesive with accurate date of opening or failure to discard eyelash adhesive container within 30 days of opening;
Any other sanitation violation— $50;
Unauthorized services:
Massage — $300;
Microdermabrasion — formal hearing;
Lasers — formal hearing;
Chemical peels that penetrate the skin beyond the epidermis — formal hearing;
Practice causing tissue destruction or penetration to the blood fluid barrier — formal hearing;
Credo blade — $300;
Prohibited practices:
Services on client with infectious or contagious disease — formal hearing;
Operator with infectious or contagious disease performing services — formal hearing;
Services on inflamed skin or scalp without physician authorization — formal hearing;
Removal of corns, calluses, or other growths of skin by cutting — $300;
Use of electrical muscle stimulator devices — $300;
Cosmetic tattooing — formal hearing;
Presence of an animal other than a designated service animal — $100;
Sale of used hairpieces — $300;
Methyl methacrylate liquid monomer (MMA) — formal hearing;
Interference with inspector — formal hearing.
An applicant or licensee shall respond in writing to a written communication from the Board within 30 days of the date of mailing.
The Board shall send a written communication by first-class mail to the last address furnished to the Board by an applicant or licensee.
It is a responsibility of an applicant or licensee to notify the Board in writing if there has been a change in the applicant’s or licensee’s address.
Failure to respond as required by this regulation may be considered by the Board to be a violation of Business Occupations and Professions Article, §5-314(a)(1)(viii), Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Prohibitions.
section.text: The following activities are prohibited in any full service or limited practice salon or in regard to any salon-sponsored services:

para.text: The performance of services of any kind on a client who has an infectious or contagious disease that presents a hazard to others;
The performance of services of any kind by a licensee or registrant who has an infectious or contagious disease that presents a hazard to clients;

The performance of services of any kind upon the surface of a client's skin, scalp, or nail where the skin, scalp, or nail is inflamed or where a skin infection or eruption is present unless authorized by a physician;
The removal of corns, calluses, or other growths of the skin, such as warts, by cutting;
The use of electrical muscle stimulator devices purported to produce nonsurgical face or body lifts;
Cosmetic tattooing;
The presence of a dog, cat, bird, or any kind of animal on the salon premises with the exception of a designated service animal;
The sale of any hairpieces which previously have been worn including, but not limited to wigs, toupees, wiglets, falls, and switches;
The use or possession of a Credo blade or a similar razor-type implement used to cut growths on the skin;
Unless an individual possesses a valid and appropriate health occupation license issued by the Maryland Department of Health:
The use of lasers, microdermabrasion equipment, or any other mechanical device used to remove one or more layers of skin;
The use of any product or method that causes tissue destruction or penetrates the blood fluid barrier;
Chemical peels that penetrate the skin beyond the epidermis;
Mechanical tissue alteration, including, but not limited to:
Microdermabrasion; and
Electrotherapy;
Contouring or sculpting using cryotherapy, radio frequency, or liposuction;
Any procedure causing ablation of the dermis or hypodermis; and
Any form of skin injections.

In addition to the sanitation requirements in Regulation .04 of this chapter, all establishments that offer the services normally performed by an esthetician shall comply with the sanitation requirements in §B of this regulation.
Sanitation Requirements.
Creams, lotions, powders, and other cosmetics shall be removed from the patron by means of disposable absorbent cotton, cleansing tissue, cotton swab, pledget, or other similar material.
Lip and eyebrow pencils shall be sharpened after each use.
Lip color, eye color, shadows, or other cosmetics shall be removed from the container by a spatula and applied to the client with a disposable or cleansed and sanitized applicator.
Disposable lip, makeup, eyelash, or other cosmetic applicator shall be discarded immediately after use.
Hair removal waxes may not be used for more than one client. Any excess wax left after client service shall be discarded immediately.
Extraction lancets shall be cleaned and disinfected immediately after each use.
An EPA-registered disinfectant effective against HIV and hepatitis viruses or a hospital-grade tuberculocidal disinfectant shall be:
Kept on the esthetician tray for contact disinfection of implements that may come in contact with blood; and
Changed after each use.
Nondisposable drapings shall be laundered after each client.
Microneedling.
New needles must be used for each client.
Needles must be discarded into a sharps container.
Reusable equipment must be cleaned and disinfected after each client.

In addition to the sanitation requirements in Regulation .04 of this chapter, all establishments offering the services normally performed by eyelash extension technicians shall comply with the requirements of this regulation.
Sanitation Requirements.
If linens are used, clean linens must be used for each client.
Hands must be washed immediately before and after serving each client.

Chairs and beds, including headrests, must be cleaned and disinfected after providing services to each client. Chairs and beds must be made of or covered in a non-porous material that can be disinfected.
Adhesive palettes must be disposed of after each use.
When a container of adhesive used for eyelash extensions is first opened, it must be labeled with the date of opening, and such containers must be disposed of within 30 days of opening.
Premade fans must not be reused.
Disposable tools must not be reused.
Gel pads and tape used to secure lower lashes must be disposed of after each use.
Tape used must be medical grade.

section.heading: Citations to Beauty Salons.
section.text: Unless otherwise indicated, the following civil penalties are applicable to the following violations of the laws and regulations of the Board of Cosmetologists discovered by a Board inspector during an inspection of a beauty salon:

para.text: Operating a beauty salon for which a permit:
Never has been issued — formal hearing;
Expired — $300;
Licensing of personnel:
Operating without a valid license — formal hearing;
Services beyond scope — $300;
Improper supervision of apprentice — $300;
No valid government-issued photo identification — $100;
Failure to display:
Sign — $25;
Permit — $50;
Condensed rules and regulations — $100;
Zoning certificate — $50;
Sanitation, unauthorized services, prohibited practices, and interference with inspector. The civil penalties applicable to licensees under COMAR 09.22.01.17C-F apply to beauty salons.

section.heading: Sanitation Requirements-Eyelash Extensions.
section.text: In addition to the sanitation requirements in Regulation .03 of this chapter, a cosmetology school approved by the State Board of Education or the Maryland Higher Education Commission that offers cosmetology services normally performed by an eyelash extension technician shall comply with the requirements of COMAR 09.22.02.07B.

section.heading: Complaints and Violations in Beauty Schools.

para.text: This regulation applies whenever a complaint is made to the Board by an inspector, member of the Board, or any other person in regard to the sanitary condition of a cosmetology school or the sanitary practice of cosmetology performed at a cosmetology school.

Upon receipt of a complaint, in compliance with Business Occupations and Professions Article, §5-509, Annotated Code of Maryland, an inspector shall investigate the complaint, and the Board shall determine if the complaint alleges grounds adequate to warrant action by the Board.
If the Board determines that the complaint does not allege grounds adequate to warrant action by the Board, the Board shall dismiss the complaint.

If the Board determines that the complaint does allege grounds adequate to warrant action by the Board, the Board shall notify the owner of the school, or an authorized representative of the school, in writing, and provide the owner of the school with an opportunity to correct the violation.
If the owner corrects each alleged violation within 10 days of the written notification, the Board shall dismiss the complaint and notify the owner.

If the owner fails to correct each alleged violation within 10 days of the written notification, the Board shall institute and conduct a hearing as provided in State Government Article, Title 10, Subtitle 2, Annotated Code of Maryland.
If the Board conducts a hearing and concludes that the owner has violated one or more sanitation regulations, the Board may impose a fine not to exceed $300 for each violation.

The Board shall report the sanitation violation and the amount of fine imposed to the Department of Education in regard to a public school or to the Maryland Higher Education Commission in regard to a private career school.

section.heading: Citations to Schools.
section.text: Unless otherwise indicated, the civil penalties applicable to licensees under COMAR 09.22.01.17C—F apply to schools.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Approved continuing education course” means a course approved by the Maryland Board of Cosmetologists.
“Board” means the Maryland Board of Cosmetologists.
“Classroom instruction” means the art or science of teaching in a space:
That is exclusively used for teaching during periods of instruction;
That is conducive to learning; and
Where an assigned instructor is present.
Contact Hour
“Contact hour” means a period of 60 minutes in which actual learning takes place.
“Contact hour” does not include
A portion of the program devoted to registration, breaks, or meals; or
Fractions of an hour.
“Continuing education course” means the basic measurement for a licensee’s direct participation in continuing education, consisting of 6 contact hours.
“Continuing education course related to cosmetology” means a course that covers one or more of the following topics:
Board Laws and Regulations (1 to 2 hours);
Elective topics (1 to 4 hours);
Health (1 to 2 hours);
Safety (1 to 2 hours); or
Welfare (1 to 2 hours).
“Course” means classroom or distance learning instruction of 1 clock hour, but not more than 6 clock hours.
“Course provider” means someone who is qualified to conduct continuing education courses in accordance with Business Occupations and Professional Article, §5-205(c)(2), Annotated Code of Maryland.
Distance Learning Instruction.
“Distance learning instruction” means the art or science of teaching where:
The instructor and student may be separated by distance and time;
Equipment may be needed to communicate the content; or
The content is specifically developed for this form of instruction.
“Distance learning instruction” includes any other delivery system approved by the Board.
“Instructor” means an individual engaged by a course provider to teach a continuing education course.
“License” means, unless the context requires otherwise, a license issued by the Board.
“Licensee” means an individual awarded a license by the Board.
The Board may approve continuing education courses that include, but are not limited to, the following topics:
Business;
Communicable and infectious disease control;
The practice of cosmetology, esthetics, eyelash extension techniques, and nail techniques;
Ethics and standards of professional practice;
Occupational health and safety;
Product knowledge;
Sanitation; and
State of Maryland laws and regulations.
The subject matter of the course shall meet the following requirements:
Address the protection of health and safety of the general public;
Enable the licensee to serve the objectives of the consumer in a cosmetology transaction; or
Enable the licensee to develop competency and professionalism in a changing marketplace.
The course shall meet the following criteria:
Cover the topic area cited in the applications;
Have an outline detailing the sequence of topics and the amount of time allotted to each topic;
Content may not be materially changed without prior written approval of the Board; and
Consist of at least 1 clock hour.

para.text: The purpose of this chapter is to establish regulatory provisions related to the State income tax credit program for the following qualified health care practitioners who serve as preceptors without compensation in health care workforce shortage areas:
Physicians;
Advanced practice registered nurses;
Physician assistants;
Registered nurses; and
Licensed practical nurses.
This chapter describes the procedures used by the Maryland Department of Health to assess eligibility of applicants, process applications, and certify income tax credits for approved applications.
In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Applicant” means an individual:
Who has submitted an application to the Department requesting State income tax credit; and
Whose status for receipt of a tax credit certification has not yet been determined.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Health Care Practitioner” means an individual who is:
A health care practitioner, as defined in Tax-General Article, §10–752, Annotated Code of Maryland;
An advanced practice registered nurse as defined in Health Occupations Article, §8-101, Annotated Code of Maryland;
A registered nurse as defined in Health Occupations Article, §8-101, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
A licensed practical nurse as defined in Health Occupations Article, §8-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Health care workforce shortage area” means:
A health professional shortage area;
A medically underserved area;
A medically underserved population;
Federally designated rural areas;
State defined shortage areas;
State defined rural areas; or
Other areas defined as in need by the Secretary of Health or Governor or as otherwise determined by the Department in consultation with the Governor’s Workforce Development Board.
“Income tax credit” means a nonrefundable credit against State income tax.
“Preceptor” means a health care practitioner participating in a preceptorship program.
“Preceptorship program” has the meaning as stated in Tax-General Article, §§10–738, and 10-752, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Rotation” means a minimum of:
100 hours of community-based clinical training between a:
Medical student and a licensed physician preceptor;
Physician assistant student and a licensed physician, nurse practitioner, or physician assistant preceptor in family medicine, general internal medicine, or general pediatrics practice or setting; or
90 hours of community-based clinical training between a:
Nurse practitioner student and a licensed physician or nurse practitioner preceptor;
A registered nurse student and an advanced practice registered nurse or registered nurse preceptor; or
A licensed practical nurse student and an advanced practice registered nurse, registered nurse, or licensed practical nurse preceptor.
“Taxable year” means the annual accounting period for keeping records and reporting income and expenses that starts on January 1 and ends on December 31 of the same year.

section.heading: Program Eligibility.
section.text: A healthcare practitioner preceptor in a preceptorship program is eligible for an income tax credit if the individual served:

para.text: Without compensation for a minimum of three rotations; and
In a health care workforce shortage area.
Applicant.
By the published deadlines, on the form designated by the Department, an applicant shall:
Submit a complete application to the Department;
Facilitate submission of application components by all contributors; and
Provide any supporting documentation as requested by the Department.
An applicant may voluntarily withdraw the application without prejudice.
An applicant may submit a new application subject to §B of this regulation.
The Department shall receive applications by January 5 for the previous taxable year during which rotations were completed.
The Department shall:
Accept applications received during the application period for Department review;
Approve the application if the:
Application is complete;
Applicant is determined to be eligible; and
Funds are available in the appropriate Fund based on precepted student type;
Disapprove an application if the application:
Fails to provide sufficient information or comply with requested documentation to determine eligibility;
Has the appropriate documentation but is determined to be ineligible; or
Does not satisfy the requirements established by law or the Department; and
Provide the applicant with written notice of the final disposition of the application within 45 calendar days of receipt of an application.
Appeal of Disapproval of Application.

An applicant may appeal the decision to disapprove an application by submitting an appeal request in writing to the Department within 30 calendar days of the date on the notice of the disapproved application.
If an applicant submits an appeal, the Department, within 60 calendar days of receipt of the appeal, shall:
Sustain or reverse the decision on the application; and
Provide written notice to the applicant:
Indicating whether the decision on the application has been sustained or reversed; and
Providing an explanation of the decision.

An applicant may request a hearing within 10 calendar days after the date of written notice of the Department’s disapproval of an application as stated in §D(2) of this regulation. Following receipt of the request, the Department shall follow hearing procedures in accordance with COMAR 10.01.03.
Failure to be present for the hearing without prior notice, communication, or approval from the Department may result in:
The appeal nullified and decision sustained;
The hearing on the appeal not being rescheduled; or
Appropriate sanctions as determined by the Department.

section.heading: Verification and Audit of Income Tax Credits.

para.text: The Department may:
Request from an applicant additional information to verify statements in an application; and

Use independent verification, such as a preceptor list and preceptor completion letters received by the medical and nursing schools, to verify information reported on an application for an income tax credit.
The Comptroller shall retain its audit authority under Tax-General Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.

On approval of an application, the Department shall issue an income tax credit certificate to the approved healthcare practitioner in the amount of $1,000 for each qualifying student rotation for which the healthcare practitioner served as a preceptor.
Income tax credits shall be issued for the taxable year of the application during which the health care practitioner served without compensation.
The total amount of an income tax credit allowed for an individual in a taxable year may not exceed the:
Maximum credit amount as indicated in Tax-General Article, §§10–738, 10-739, 10-739.1, and 10-752, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
State income tax imposed for that individual for that taxable year.

Any unused amount of an income tax credit for a taxable year may not be carried over to any other taxable year by the preceptor, as referenced in Tax-General Article, §§10–738, 10-739, 10-739.1, and 10-752, Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Responsibilities of the Comptroller.
section.text: On notification that an income tax credit certificate has been issued by the Department, the Comptroller shall transfer an amount equal to the income tax credit amount stated in the income tax credit certificate from the Nurse Practitioner Preceptorship Tax Credit Fund, the Physician Assistant Preceptorship Tax Credit Fund, or Licensed Practical Nurse and Registered Nurse Preceptorship Tax Credit Fund, to the General Fund.

para.text: The Department shall issue final income tax credits based on available funds.
This chapter remains in effect until:
The date that funding for income tax credits ceases to be available; or
Sunset on June 30, 2030.

annotations.annotation: Tax-General Article, §§10-738, 10-739, 10-739-1, and 10.752, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: January 29, 2018 (45:2 Md. R. 68)
Chapter revised effective September 19, 2022 (49:19 Md. R. 868)
Chapter revised effective March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 184)

section.heading: Scope.
section.text: This chapter applies to all petitions submitted to the Department to promulgate, amend, or repeal a regulation over which the Secretary has rulemaking authority in accordance with State Government Article, §10-122, Annotated Code of Maryland..

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Petition” means a petition to promulgate, amend, or repeal a regulation.
“Petitioner” means an interested person filing a petition to the Department to promulgate, amend, or repeal a regulation.
“Regulation” has the meaning stated in State Government Article, §10-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health.
A petition shall be in writing and include:
A detailed description of the regulation which the petitioner requests the Department to promulgate, amend, or repeal;
If a request for amendment or repeal, a citation to the affected regulation;
A statement in support of the petition; and
The petitioner’s:
Name;
Signature;
Address; and
Telephone number.
A petition shall be submitted by the petitioner to the Director of the Department’s Office of Regulation and Policy Coordination via email or U.S. Mail.
Disposition of Petition. Within 60 days after receipt of a petition, the Secretary shall:
Deny the petition; or
Initiate rulemaking proceedings.
Notification Requirements.
If the Secretary denies the petition, the Secretary shall provide in writing the reason for denial in accordance with Regulation .06 of this chapter.
If the Secretary initiates rulemaking proceedings, the Secretary shall provide in writing notification to the petitioner:
Upon the intent to initiate rulemaking proceedings;
Upon publication of the proposed action in the Maryland Register; and
Upon final disposition through either publication of a notice of final action in the Maryland Register or discontinuation of rulemaking.
If the Secretary discontinues rulemaking proceedings the written notification provided to the petitioner in accordance with §B(2) of this regulation shall state the reasons for discontinuation.
The Secretary shall provide the written notification required under §B of this regulation to any other individual requesting notification.

section.heading: Reasons for Denial of a Petition.
section.text: The Secretary may deny a petition for any reason permitted by law, including, but not limited to:

para.text: That the Secretary does not have the authority to grant the petition;
That the Department does not have adequate information or resources to evaluate the petition;
That the Department does not have adequate resources to implement the requested regulation if it is adopted;

That the Secretary previously adopted a regulation dealing with the same subject matter as that of the petition and rejected the position taken by petitioner at that time, or the petitioner unreasonably failed to make his position known at that time; or
That the requested regulation change is contrary to or inconsistent with Departmental policy.
Rulemaking proceedings may include any type of action which the Secretary is authorized or required by law to take when promulgating, amending, or repealing regulations, including:
Studies to gather information concerning the regulation;
Advance notice of proposed rulemaking if the Secretary determines that additional information or public comment is necessary or desirable before a proposed regulation can be formulated;
Referral to advisory councils or other official or quasi-official agencies or groups responsible for reviewing the regulation;
Proposal of the regulation in the Maryland Register;

Reproposal of the regulation if the Secretary determines that, before final adoption, the proposed regulation should be altered and any part of the proposed text differs substantively from its original text; or;
Modification of the regulation change either in form or in substance, and either before or after proposal in the Maryland Register, to better reflect the Secretary's responsibilities.
Despite the initiation of rulemaking proceedings, the Secretary may decide not to promulgate, amend, or repeal the regulation.
Upon final disposition, the Secretary shall provide the notification required to the petitioner in accordance with Regulation .05 of this chapter.

section.heading: Files of Petitions for Regulations.
section.text: The Department shall maintain a file of all petitions submitted and their disposition. This file shall be available for public inspection in accordance with General Provisions Article, §§4-201—4-206, Annotated Code of Maryland.
