container.heading: Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Physicians and Physician Assistants

section.heading: Purpose.
section.text: The purpose of the Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Physicians and Physician Assistants is to improve health care access for all Marylanders by recruiting and retaining health care providers in areas of Maryland with health care professional shortages through education loan repayments assistance to program participants.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Education loan” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24-1701, Annotated Code of Maryland. ​
“Eligible field of employment” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24–1704, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Full-time” means an individual’s work week consists of 40 or more hours at an eligible practice site.
“Guidelines” means the Maryland Loan Repayment Programs Guidelines and any subregulatory guidance established by the Department.

“Medical resident” means an individual who has completed medical school and is participating in a post-graduate residency education program accredited by the Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education, or its successor, or the American Osteopathic Association, or its successor, who is not licensed to practice medicine in the State.
“Participant” means an individual who has been offered a Program award and is under a service obligation with the Program.
“Part-time” means an individual’s work week consists of between 20 to 39 hours at an eligible practice site.
“Physician” has the meaning stated in Health-Occupations Article, §14-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, who practices medicine either full-time or part-time.
“Physician assistant” has the meaning stated in Health-Occupations Article, §15-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, who practices full-time or part-time.
“Practice site” means a site where a participant provides clinical services and fulfills their service obligation.
“Primary care” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24-1701, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Program” means the Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Physicians and Physician Assistants.

“Promissory Note” means a contractual agreement between a participant and the Department describing disbursement methods, participant roles and responsibilities, compliance penalties, and other terms as determined by the Department.

“Service Obligation Agreement” means a contractual agreement between a participant and the Department specifying participant service obligations, total and annual Program award amounts, required data and verifications, penalties for breach, and other terms as determined by the Department.
Eligibility. To be eligible for the program the individual shall:
Meet the licensure requirements set forth in §B of this regulation;
Be a United States citizen or national;
Be liable and personally owe educational debt;
Have a valid employment contract with an eligible practice site in the State that will be in effect by the start of any service obligation to be fulfilled;
Provide evidence of practice as either part-time or full-time at that eligible practice site;
Practice full-time or part-time providing health care services at an eligible practice site, with at least 80 percent of time spent in direct patient care for a minimum of 45 weeks per year; and
Utilize Program funds for the repayment of eligible education loans.
Licensure Requirements. To be eligible for the program, an individual shall be one of the following:
A physician possessing active and valid licensure without restrictions or encumbrances to practice Allopathic Medicine in the State;
A physician possessing active and valid licensure without restrictions to or encumbrances to practice Osteopathic Medicine in the State;
A physician assistant possessing active and valid licensure without restriction or encumbrances in the State; or
A final year medical resident who is specializing in primary care and overseen by a postgraduate training program in the State.
Eligibility Restrictions. An individual may not be eligible for the Program if they have:
A federal or state judgment lien;
Defaulted on any federal or state payment obligations even if the governmental creditor now considers them to be in good standing;
Breached a prior service obligation to federal, state, or local government or other entity, even if the obligation is subsequently satisfied;
Any federal or non-federal debt written off as uncollectible or received a waiver of any service or payment obligation;

Any outstanding contractual obligations for a health care professional service to the federal government, another state loan repayment program, or other entity unless those service obligations are completely satisfied prior to the commencement of the Program service obligation; and
Received the two maximum awards for service obligations to the Program.
Eligible Practice Sites. An eligible practice site shall:
Be an eligible field of employment in accordance with Health-General Article, §24–1704, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Be physically located in the State;
Provide services to all patients regardless of their ability to pay; and
If the practice site charges for health care professional services:
Charge at the usual and customary prevailing rate;
Utilize a financial aid policy of discounts or sliding fee scale for individuals with limited incomes; and
Accept reimbursement from Medicare, the Maryland Medical Assistance Program, and the Maryland Children’s Health Insurance Program, if applicable to the patient type.
By the published deadlines, on the form designated by the Department, an applicant shall:
Submit a complete application to the Department;
Facilitate submission of application components by all contributors; and
Submit any supporting documentation as requested by the Department.
The Department shall disapprove an application if the application:
Fails to provide sufficient information or comply with requested documentation;
Has the appropriate documentation but is determined to be ineligible; or
Does not satisfy the requirements established by law or the Department.
The Department shall:
Assess applicant eligibility based on documentation received by the published deadline;
Require from an applicant additional documentation or use independent verification means to verify information in the application; and
Score an eligible application based on priority criteria in accordance with:
Health-General Article, §24–1705, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
The guidelines established by the Department.
An applicant shall respond to an award offer by submitting all required documentation to the Department within 90 calendar days of notice of award.
Failure of the applicant to provide required documentation shall result in:
An application determined as ineligible; or
Cancellation of any offered awards.
Appeal of Disapproval of Application.

An applicant may appeal the decision to disapprove an application by submitting an appeal request in writing to the Department within 30 calendar days of the date on the notice of the disapproved application.
If an applicant submits an appeal, the Department, within 60 calendar days, shall:
Sustain or reverse the decision on the application; and
Provide written notice to the applicant:
Indicating whether the decision on the application has been sustained or reversed; and
Providing an explanation of the decision.

An applicant may request a hearing within 10 calendar days after the date of written notice of the Department’s disapproval of an application as stated in §D(2) of this regulation. When the Department receives the request, it will follow hearing procedures in accordance with COMAR 10.01.03.
Failure to be present for the hearing without prior notice, communication, or approval from the Department may result in:
The appeal nullified and decision sustained;
Hearing on the appeal not rescheduled; or
Appropriate sanctions taken as deemed by the Department.
The distribution of Program awards shall be based on the funding prioritization in accordance with Health-General Article, §24–1704(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Department shall prioritize funding for the repayment of education loans in accordance with Health-General Article, §24–1705(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Department shall annually determine, based on the availability of funds:
The maximum number of participants for the Program in each priority area; and
The minimum and maximum amount of loan repayment assistance awarded.
The amount of award offered and paid shall not exceed the lesser of:
The loan repayment amount specified in the Service Obligation Agreement; or
A participant's total verified debt from eligible education loans.
A participant shall provide the Department with required documentation including:
Lender verification of education loan balance;
Documentation of employer verification;
A signed Promissory Note that includes:
The disbursement methods utilized by the Department in the repayment of education loans;
The roles and responsibilities of the participant in the disbursement of funds to the lender institution;
Penalties for failing to comply with terms and conditions of agreements with the Department; and
Other terms as determined by the Department;
A Service Obligation Agreement that includes:
Obligations of the participant;
Total and annual amounts of the Program award;
Completion of data and verifications required by the Department;
Penalties for breach of the Promissory Note and Service Obligation Agreements;
Incorporation of the Promissory Note and Program Guidelines; and
Other appropriate terms as determined by the Department; and
All other documentations as requested by the Department.
A participant shall:
Continue to meet the eligibility requirements as stated in Regulation .03A and B of this chapter; and
Serve a minimum service obligation as stipulated in the Service Obligation Agreement with the Department.
A final year medical resident shall:
Meet all requirements set forth in §A of this regulation;
Complete residency training in primary care, and begin their service obligation without interruption; and
Fulfill all requirements of §B of this regulation once the service obligation commences.
The Department shall verify annually during Program participation, the participant’s compliance with:
The Program Promissory Note;
The Service Obligation Agreement; and
Any subregulatory guidelines developed by the Department.
A participant shall respond to annual verification by submitting all required documentation to the Department within 180 calendar days of request.
The Department shall annually receive confirmation from the:
Approved practice site that the participant provided services in the previous year and continues to practice at the practice site; and
Lender institution that the participant has an outstanding education loan balance that is not in default.
For a medical resident in their final year of medical residence, prior to the disbursement of award payment for the first obligation cycle, the Department shall:
Receive confirmation from the residency training program that the participant is specializing in primary care; and
Follow the same verification procedures stated in §§A and B of this regulation once service obligations by the participant commences.
Failure to provide required reverification documentation to the Department will constitute a breach of agreement with the Department.
A participant's failure to comply with the Service Obligation Agreement and Promissory Note shall constitute a breach of agreement with the Department.

A final year medical resident who fails to begin a service obligation at the start of the next obligation cycle following the completion of primary care residency training, shall be in breach of the Service Obligation Agreement and Promissory Note.
Penalties for breach of agreement with the Department shall be stated in the Service Obligation Agreement and Promissory Note, and may include:
Financial penalties dependent on the funding source of the award provided to the participant;
The elimination of future eligibility for the Program; or
Referral of the participant to the Maryland Board of Physicians for investigation of the participant's violation of breach of service obligation.

section.heading: Amendments to a Participant’s Service Obligation.

para.text: Practice Site Transfer.
The Department may consider practice site transfer requests during a participant’s service obligation period.

A participant shall submit a site transfer request to the Department for consideration and receive approval of the site transfer from the Department prior to a participant’s separation from an approved practice site.
A site transfer request may not be submitted until the second service obligation year.

The Department may consider deferment of a participant’s service obligation or payment obligation for up to 1 year, if determined by the Department that compliance by the participant with the terms and conditions of the Service Obligation Agreement would involve extreme hardship as evidenced through documentation provided to the Department.
Any request by a participant for service obligation amendments shall follow the written process and procedures as established by the Department in subregulatory guidance.
A decision made by the Department upon request for a service obligation amendment shall be final.
The Department may consider cancellation of a participant service obligation with evidence under the following circumstances:
Illness or injury that prohibits future possibility of service; or
Death as determined by a death certificate or other conclusive evidence of death under State law.

annotations.annotation: Health-General Article, §24–1701—24-1708, ; and Health Occupations Article, §§14–207 and 15–206(c), Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 184)

container.heading: Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Nurses and Nursing Support Staff

section.heading: Purpose.
section.text: The purpose of the Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Nurses and Nursing Support Staff is to improve access to care for all Marylanders by recruiting and retaining health care providers in health care professional shortage areas of Maryland through the provision of education loan repayment assistance to eligible participants in exchange for their service obligations.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Education loan” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24-1901, Annotated Code of Maryland. ​
“Eligible field of employment” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24–1904, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Full-time” means an individual’s work week consists of 40 or more hours at an eligible practice site.
“Guidelines” means the Maryland Loan Repayment Programs Guidelines and any subregulatory guidance established by the Department.
“Nurse” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §24–1901, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Nursing Support Staff” has the meaning stated in Health-General-Article, §24–1901, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Participant” means an individual who has been offered a Program award and is under a service obligation.
“Part-time” means an individual’s work week consists of between 20 to 39 hours at an eligible practice site.
“Practice site” means a site where a participant provides clinical services and fulfills their service obligation.
“Program” means the Maryland Loan Assistance Repayment Program for Nurses and Nursing Support Staff.

“Promissory Note” means a contractual agreement between a participant and the Department describing disbursement methods, participant roles and responsibilities, compliance penalties, and other terms as determined by the Department.

“Service Obligation Agreement” means a contractual agreement between a participant and the Department specifying participant service obligations, total and annual Program award amounts, required data and verifications, penalties for breach, and other terms as determined by the Department.
Eligibility. To be eligible for the Program, a nurse or nursing support staff shall:
Be liable and personally owe education loan debt;
Be a United States citizen or national;
Have a valid employment contract with an eligible practice site in the State that will be in effect by the start of any service obligation to be fulfilled;
Provide evidence of practice as either part-time or full-time at the eligible practice site;

Practice full-time or part-time providing health care services at an eligible practice site, with at least eighty percent of time spent in direct patient care for at least a minimum of 45 weeks per year;
Utilize Program funds for the repayment of eligible education loans; and
Possess an active and valid certificate or license to practice nursing or fulfill a nursing support staff role in the State, without restrictions or encumbrances.
Eligibility Restrictions. To be eligible for the Program, a nurse or nursing support staff may not have:
Federal or State judgment liens;
Defaulted on any federal or State payment obligations even if the governmental creditor now considers them to be in good standing;
Breached a prior service obligation to federal, State, or local government or any other entity, even if the obligation is subsequently satisfied;
Any federal or non-federal debt written off as uncollectible or received a waiver of any service or payment obligation;

Any outstanding contractual obligations for health care professional service to the federal government, another state loan repayment program, or other entities unless those service obligations are completely satisfied prior to the commencement of the Program service obligation; and
Received the two maximum awards for service obligations to the Program.
Eligible Practice Sites. An eligible practice site shall:
Be an eligible field of employment in accordance with Health-General Article, §24–1904, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Be physically located in the State;
Provide services to all patients regardless of their ability to pay;
If the practice site charges for health care professional services:
Charge at the usual and customary prevailing rate;
Utilize a financial aid policy of discounts or sliding fee scale for individuals with limited incomes; and
Accept reimbursement from Medicare, the Maryland Medical Assistance Program, and the Maryland Children’s Health Insurance Program, if applicable to the patient type.
By the published deadline, on the form designated by the Department, an applicant shall:
Submit a complete application to the Department;
Facilitate submission of application components by all contributors; and
Submit any supporting documentation as requested by the Department.
The Department shall disapprove an application if the application:
Fails to provide sufficient information or comply with requested documentation;
Has the appropriate documentation but is determined to be ineligible; or
Does not satisfy the requirements established by law or the Department.
The Department shall:
Assess applicant eligibility based on documentation received by the published deadline;
Require from an applicant additional documentation or use independent verification means to verify information in the application; and
Score an eligible application based on priority criteria in accordance with:
Health-General Article, §24–1905, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
The guidelines established by the Department.
An applicant shall respond to an award offer by submitting all required documentation to the Department within 90 calendar days of notice of award.
Failure of the applicant to provide required documentation shall result in:
An application determined as ineligible; or
Cancellation of any offered award.
Appeal of Disapproval of Application.

An applicant may appeal the decision to disapprove an application by submitting an appeal request in writing to the Department within 30 calendar days of the date on the notice of the disapproved application.
If an applicant submits an appeal, the Department, within 60 calendar days, shall:
Sustain or reverse the decision on the application; and
Provide written notice to the applicant:
Indicating whether the decision on the application has been sustained or reversed; and
Providing an explanation of the decision.

An applicant may request a hearing within 10 calendar days after the date of written notice of the Department’s disapproval of an application as stated in §D(2) of this regulation. When the Department receives the request, it will follow hearing procedures in accordance with COMAR 10.01.03.
Failure to be present for the hearing without prior notice, communication, or approval from the Department may result in:
The appeal nullified and decision sustained;
Hearing on the appeal not rescheduled; or
Appropriate sanctions taken as deemed by the Department.
The distribution of program awards shall be based on the funding prioritization in accordance with Health-General Article, §24-1904.1(a), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Department shall prioritize funding for the repayment of education loans in accordance with Health-General Article, §24–1904.1(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Department shall annually determine, based on the availability of funds:
The maximum number of participants for the Program in each priority area; and
The minimum and maximum amount of loan repayment assistance awarded.
The amount of award offered and paid shall not exceed the lesser of:
The loan repayment amount specified in the Service Obligation Agreement; or
A participant's total debt from eligible education loans.
A participant shall provide the Department with required documentation including:
Lender verification of education loan balance;
Documentation of employer verification;
A signed Promissory Note that includes;
The disbursement methods utilized by the Department in the repayment of education loans;
The roles and responsibilities of the participant in the disbursement of funds to the lender institution;
Penalties for failing to comply with terms and conditions of agreements with the Department; and
Other terms as determined by the Department;
A Service Obligation Agreement that includes;
Obligations of the participant;
Total and annual amounts of the Program award;
Completion of data and verifications required by the Department;
Penalties for breach of the Promissory Note and Service Obligation Agreements;
Incorporation of the Promissory Note and Program Guidelines; and
Other appropriate terms as determined by the Department; and
All other documentation as required by the Department.
A participant shall:
Continue to meet the eligibility requirements as stated in Regulation 03A and B of this chapter; and
Serve a minimum service obligation as stipulated in the Service Obligation Agreement with the Department.
The Department shall verify annually during Program participation, the participant’s compliance with:
The Program Promissory Note;
The Service Obligation Agreement; and
Any subregulatory guidelines developed by the Department.
A participant shall respond to annual verification by submitting all required documentation to the Department within 180 calendar days of request.
The Department shall annually receive confirmation from the:
Approved practice site that the participant provided services in the previous year and continues to practice at the practice site; and
Lender institution that the participant has an outstanding education loan balance that is not in default.
Failure to provide required reverification documentation to the Department will constitute a breach of agreement with the Department.
A participant's failure to comply with the Service Obligation Agreement and Promissory Note shall constitute a breach of agreement with the Department.
Penalties for breach of agreement with the Department shall be stated in the Service Obligation Agreement and Promissory Note, and may include:
Financial penalties dependent on the funding source of the award provided to the participant;
The elimination of future eligibility for the Program; or
Referral of the participant to the Maryland Board of Nursing for further disciplinary actions.

section.heading: Amendments to a Participant’s Service Obligation.

para.text: Practice Site Transfer.
The Department may consider practice site transfer requests during a participant’s service obligation period.

A participant shall submit a site transfer request to the Department for consideration and receive approval of the site transfer from the Department prior to a participant’s separation from an approved practice site.
A site transfer request may not be submitted until the second service obligation year.

The Department may consider deferment of a participant’s service obligation or payment obligation for up to 1 year if determined by the Department that compliance by the participant with the terms and conditions of the Service Obligation Agreement would involve extreme hardship as evidenced through documentation provided to the Department.
Any request by a participant for service obligation amendments shall follow the written process and procedures as established by the Department in subregulatory guidance.
A decision made by the Department upon request for a service obligation amendment shall be final.
The Department may consider cancellation of a participant service obligation with evidence under the following circumstances:
Illness or injury that prohibits future possibility of service; or
Death as determined by a death certificate or other conclusive evidence of death under State law.

annotations.annotation: Health-General Article, §§24–1901—24-1906, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 185)

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Board” means the State Board of Pharmacy.
“Foreign school or college of pharmacy” means a school or college of pharmacy that is not located in any state of the United States.
“Licensure examinations” means the Board-administered or Board-approved examinations required of applicants for licensure which include the following:
Exam I: North American Pharmacist Licensure Examination (NAPLEX);
Part I: NAPLEX, and
Part II: Pharmacy Law Test;
Exam II: Pharmacy Law Test through either:
The Uniform Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (UMPJE) and the Maryland Pharmacy Law webinar and post-webinar test; or
The Maryland Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (MPJE); and
Exam III:
Prescreening Test of Oral English Competency; or
Test of Oral English Competency.
Examination Eligibility.

An applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam I upon satisfactory proof that the applicant has graduated, or is expected to graduate at the completion of the immediate semester, from a school or college of pharmacy that has been:
Approved by the Board; or
Accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).

Beginning May 1, 2027, an applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam II upon satisfactory proof that the applicant has graduated from, or has successfully completed the didactic portion of the core curriculum of a school or college of pharmacy that has been:
Approved by the Board; or
Accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).
An applicant may not receive a license until the applicant has:
Obtained passing scores on Exam I, Exam II, and Exam III, as applicable; and

Provided satisfactory proof to the Board of the applicant’s graduation from a college or school of pharmacy approved by the Board or accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).
Passing Scores—Exam I and Exam II.
The passing score for Exam I is 75.
II Passing Scores.
For the Maryland Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (MPJE) the passing score is 75.
For the Uniform Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (UMPJE) the passing score is 75.
Maryland Pharmacy Law Webinar and Post-Webinar Exam.
The passing score is 85.

The Maryland Pharmacy Law webinar and post-webinar exam shall provide unlimited attempts to pass the exam in one sitting provided that the applicant reviews the webinar after each failed attempt at the exam.
There may not be a fee associated with the Maryland Pharmacy Law webinar and post-webinar exam.

If a candidate receives a passing score on Exam I or Exam II, the candidate is not required to retake that exam unless the passing score was received more than 2 years before the date the candidate successfully completed all of the other examination requirements set forth in this regulation.
Passing Score—Exam III.
Prescreening Test of Oral English Competency.

On completion of a prescreening test application form provided by the Board, an applicant may elect to take a standardized prescreening test of oral English competency given by the Board's agents at times to be determined by the Board.

An agent of the Board possessing post-secondary credentials in an academic discipline acceptable to the Board shall score the prescreening test of oral English competency through the use of a standardized rating system.

An agent of the Board shall design the rating system of the prescreening test so that a passing score demonstrates that an applicant speaks fluent English as proficiently as typical United States native speakers whose English is easily understood by the average pharmacy patient or customer.

By choosing to take the prescreening test of oral English competency, an applicant is certifying to the Board that the applicant speaks English as proficiently as typical United States native speakers whose English is easily understood by the average pharmacy patient or customer.

By choosing to take the prescreening test to verify this certification, the applicant consents to pay the costs of test administration and shall pay the fee indicated on the Board's prescreening application form directly to the Board's agent.
An applicant who passes the prescreening test passes Exam II, the Test of Oral English Competency required under Regulation .02B(2)(b) of this chapter.

An applicant who fails the prescreening test for oral English competency may take the Test of Oral English Competency required under Regulation .02B(2)(b) of this chapter without prejudice to the applicant's licensure application if the applicant pays all related fees.

An applicant who has graduated from a recognized English-speaking professional school accredited by the Accreditation Council for Pharmacy Education is deemed to be proficient in the oral communication of the English language in satisfaction of requirements of the prescreening test.
Test of Oral English Competency.

In order to obtain a license to practice pharmacy, an applicant shall pass a standardized test of oral English competency approved by the Board or the board of pharmacy of another state with a score acceptable to the Board, based upon the Board's standards and the examination taken.
The Board shall provide the applicant with a list of Board-approved testing services and the passing score for each approved test of oral English competency.

Waiver. The Board shall waive the requirement as set forth under §D(2)(a) of this regulation if the applicant graduated from a recognized English-speaking professional school accredited by the Accreditation Council for Pharmacy Education.
Claims of Speech Impairment.
An applicant who claims to have a speech impairment shall submit documentation of the impairment on forms supplied by the Board and completed by a Board-approved:
Licensed physician; and
Speech-language pathologist licensed to practice speech pathology in the United States.
The applicant shall submit the documentation with:
The applicant's initial application; or
Before the applicant's second attempt at passing the test of oral English competency.
After the second examination, the applicant may not submit documentation of impairment unless:
An intervening medical or surgical event caused the impairment;
The applicant claims the impairment before a third examination; and
The applicant provides documentation that is approved by the Board.
The Board may accept documentation of a speech impairment as a passing score on Exam II if the applicant also:

Provides documentation from a Board-approved licensed pharmacist or a Board-approved instructor in a school of pharmacy that the applicant can communicate in a professionally competent manner with patients and health care providers; and

Describes, in a hearing before the Board, the manner in which the applicant would communicate with a licensed prescriber regarding a prescription and would counsel a typical patient on proper drug usage.
The certifying or verifying authority shall send all certificates and verifications directly to the Board for the applicants.
The Board-approved testing services may not accept certificates and verifications sent to or by the applicant.
If any of the documents required under this regulation are in a language other than English, the certifying or verifying authority shall submit a certified translation.
The applicant shall bear the costs of:
Translation by a certified translator;
Taking the test of oral English competency; and
Obtaining documents required under this regulation.
Graduates of a Foreign School or College of Pharmacy.
In addition to the other examination requirements set forth in this regulation, an applicant who has graduated from a foreign school or college of pharmacy shall:
Be approved to take the Foreign Pharmacy Graduate Equivalency Examination (FPGEE); and
Obtain the Foreign Pharmacy Graduate Education Committee (FPGEC) Certificate.
The passing score for the FPGEE is 75.
Upon successful completion of the FPGEE and submission of the FPGE Certificate, an applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam I and Exam II.

para.text: A school vehicle driver shall pass a physical examination before employment and once during each 12 months of employment.

The physical examination under §A of this regulation shall be conducted by a medical examiner as defined in the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations, 49 CFR §390.5, which is incorporated by reference.

A licensed optometrist may perform as much of the medical examination as pertains to visual acuity, field of vision, and ability to recognize colors as specified in 49 CFR §391.41(b)(10), which is incorporated by reference.
The medical examination shall be conducted in accordance with 49 CFR §391.43, which is incorporated by reference.

Except as provided in Regulation .01-1A of this chapter, a school vehicle driver shall meet the same physical qualifications required for drivers under 49 CFR §391.41(b), which is incorporated by reference, except that the physical disqualification waiver provisions of 49 CFR §391.49 do not apply.
The medical examiner shall:
Record the results of the medical examination on forms approved by the Administration;
Forward the results of the medical examination to the driver's employer or owner of the school vehicle; and
Complete a medical examiner's certificate for individuals qualified to drive a school vehicle.
For drivers who do not hold a Maryland license the employer or owner of the school vehicle shall:
Maintain and keep on file a copy of the driving record that has been issued within the past 12 months; and
Record the issuance date of the medical examiner’s certificate to ensure that a medical exam has occurred as required under §A of this regulation; and
Monitor the driver in a license monitoring system, if available, in the state where their license is issued.
For drivers who hold a Maryland license, the employer or owner of the school vehicle shall:
Maintain and keep on file a copy of the driving record that has been issued within the past 12 months;
Record the issuance date of the medical examiner’s certificate to ensure that a medical exam has occurred as required under §A of this regulation; and
Monitor the driver in a license monitoring system approved by the Administration.

annotations.annotation: Transportation Article, §§12-104(b) and 25-110, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: February 15, 1973
Chapter revised effective January 23, 1981 (8:2 Md. R. 116)

Regulations .01 amended and .01-1 adopted as an emergency provision effective November 20, 1987 (14:25 Md. R. 2656); emergency status expired March 18, 1988; adopted permanently effective April 4, 1988 (15:7 Md. R. 850)
Chapter revised effective July 22, 2002 (29:14 Md. R. 1075)
Regulation .01G amended effective March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 185)
Regulation .01H adopted effective March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 185)

para.text: Pathways to Initial Teacher Licenses. Applicants shall meet the requirements of one pathway to earn initial teacher licensure.
Maryland Approved Program. Teacher candidates who complete a Maryland approved educator preparation program as set forth in COMAR 13A.07.06 shall meet the following requirements:
Bachelor’s degree or higher;
Completion of an approved program as set forth in COMAR 13A.07.06;
Documentation of one of the following:
Passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a basic literacy skills assessment as approved by the State Board of Education;
Minimum overall cumulative grade point average of 3.0 on a 4.0 scale on the most recently earned degree; or
Three years of effective evaluations from a local school system in Maryland.
Passing scores as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a content licensure test approved by the State Board of Education;

Passing scores as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a reading instruction licensure test approved by the State Board of Education, or attestation of proficiency through observation completed by a Department-recognized assessor using a Department-provided observation tool; and
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a nationally recognized, portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education; or
Completion of a comprehensive local school system teacher induction program that meets the requirements listed under §B(2) of this regulation.

In-District Training Program. Teacher candidates who are hired as a teacher of record in a Maryland local school system may complete a Department-approved in-district training program. Candidates seeking licensure under the in-district pathway shall meet the following requirements:
Possession of a conditional license in the subject area and at the grade level of the license sought.
Demonstration of content knowledge by completing the following:
Bachelor’s degree or higher related to the field of the license sought;

Bachelor's degree or higher in any field and a minimum of 24 semester hours of content coursework related to the license sought, which may be completed in-person, virtually, synchronously, and/or asynchronously; or
Bachelor's degree or higher in any field and passing scores as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a content licensure test approved by the State Board of Education
Completion of a Maryland induction program under COMAR 13A.07.01 that includes:
On-site supervision and coaching;
Ongoing instructional mentoring during the induction; and
An effective, or comparable, rating on a summative evaluation of teaching performance at the end of the induction period.
Completion of a Department-approved sequence of pedagogical coursework, which may be completed in-person, virtually, synchronously, and/or asynchronously, as follows:
The teaching candidate shall ensure that this sequence of coursework is aligned to the Interstate Teacher Assessment and Support Consortium standards; and
The district and coursework provider shall agree to a memorandum of understanding.
Attestation from the district training program supervisor, or designee, school principal, and coursework provider, that the candidate is prepared for licensure.

Special Provisions. Candidates seeking licensure in the areas of elementary education, early childhood education, special education, and English to Speakers of Other Languages, shall complete additional requirements, as follows:
Candidates who fulfill §A(2)(b)(i) or (ii) of this regulation shall present 6 semester hours of coursework in each of the content areas of English, mathematics, science, and social studies; or

Candidates who fulfill §A(2)(b)(iii) of this regulation shall submit passing scores on an approved elementary assessment that includes subtests in the content areas of English, mathematics, science, and social studies, each of which equals 6 semester hours of credit in that content area; and

Passing score on a reading instruction test, approved by the State Board of Education, or provide attestation of proficiency through observation completed by a Department-recognized assessor using a Department-provided observation tool.
Experienced Nonpublic School Teacher. Experienced teacher candidates working in Maryland nonpublic schools approved under COMAR 13A.09.09 shall meet the following requirements:
Bachelor’s degree or higher related to the field of the license sought;
Verification of 5 years of effective teaching experience in the field and at the grade level of the license sought at a Maryland nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.09;
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a nationally recognized, portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education;

An effective, or comparable, rating on a year-end evaluation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system, State-operated school, or approved nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.10;
Complete a comprehensive induction program under §B(2) of this regulation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system; or
Hold a National Board Certificate from the National Board for Professional Teaching Standards; and

Special Provision. Candidates seeking licensure in the areas of elementary education, early childhood education, special education, and English to Speakers of Other Languages, shall submit of a passing score on a reading instruction test, approved by the State Board of Education, or provide attestation of proficiency through observation completed by a Department-recognized assessor using a Department-provided observation tool.
Out-of-State Teacher Preparation Program. Teacher candidates who complete a teacher preparation program in another state or foreign country shall meet the following requirements:
Bachelor’s degree or higher, or a U.S. equivalent of a foreign degree;
Complete a teacher preparation program, to include a clinical internship, approved to lead to licensure in another state or foreign country, in the license area being sought;
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a nationally recognized, portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education;

An effective, or comparable, rating on a year-end evaluation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system, State-operated school, or approved nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.10;
Complete a comprehensive induction program under §B(2) of this regulation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system; or
Hold a National Board Certificate from the National Board for Professional Teaching Standards; and

Special Provision. Candidates seeking licensure in the areas of elementary education, early childhood education, special education, and English to Speakers of Other Languages, shall submit a passing score on a reading instruction test, approved by the State Board of Education, or attestation of proficiency through observation completed by a Department-recognized assessor using a Department-provided observation tool.
Out-of-State License. Teacher candidates who hold a valid professional license/certificate from another state or foreign country shall meet the following requirements:
Bachelor’s degree or higher, or a U.S. equivalent for a foreign degree;
Valid, professional license or certificate from another state or foreign country in the license area being sought;
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education;

An effective, or comparable, rating on a year-end evaluation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system, State-operated school, or approved nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.10;
Complete a comprehensive induction program under §B(2) of this regulation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system; or
Hold a National Board Certificate from the National Board for Professional Teaching Standards; and

Special Provision. Candidates seeking licensure in the areas of elementary education, early childhood education, special education, and English to Speakers of Other Languages, shall submit a passing score on a reading instruction test, approved by the State Board of Education, or provide an attestation of proficiency through observation completed by a Department-recognized assessor using a Department-provided observation tool.
National Board Certificate. Teacher candidates who hold a National Board Certificate shall meet the following requirements:
Bachelor’s degree or higher; and
National Board Certificate issued from the National Board for Professional Teaching Standards, for which a comparable Maryland license exists.
Fine Arts Transcript Analysis. Individuals with content expertise in the areas of art, dance, music, or theater shall:
Obtain a bachelor’s degree or higher in the fine arts content area being sought;

Obtain a bachelor’s degree or higher in a fine arts area related to the area of licensure being sought, and 3 years of satisfactory content-related occupational experience, which may include satisfactory post-secondary teaching experience; or
Obtain a bachelor’s degree or higher in any field with:
At least 24 semester hours of content coursework in the fine arts license area sought; or

A passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a content assessment approved by the State Board of Education and 3 years of content-related occupational experience, which may include satisfactory post-secondary teaching experience in the area to be taught;

Complete 21 semester hours of credits of professional education coursework from an institution of higher education or through Department-approved continuing professional development credits, to include 3 credits for each of the following:
Methods of teaching;
Introduction to special education;
Human growth and development;
Assessment;
Literacy in the content area;
Culturally responsive teaching/multicultural education/strategies for multilingual learners; and
Classroom management; and
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Obtain a passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a nationally recognized, portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education;

Obtain effective, or comparable, ratings on two, year-end evaluations if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system, State-operated school, or approved nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.10; or
Complete a comprehensive induction program under §B(2) of this regulation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system.
Secondary Content Transcript Analysis. To earn a 7-12 grade band license, individuals with secondary content expertise shall meet the following requirements:
Obtain a bachelor’s degree or higher:
In the content area being sought;
In any field with a minimum of 24 semester hours of content coursework in the area of license sought;

In any field with 3 years of content-related occupational experience and a passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a content assessment approved by the State Board of Education; or
In any field with 3 years of experience teaching at an accredited institution of higher education in the content area sought; and

Complete 21 semester hours of credits of professional education coursework from an institution of higher education or through Department-approved continuing professional development credits, to include 3 credits for each of the following:
Methods of teaching;
Introduction to special education;
Human growth and development;
Assessment;
Literacy in the content area;
Culturally responsive teaching/multicultural education/strategies for multilingual Learners; and
Classroom management; and
Meet one of the following qualifications:
Obtain a passing score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a nationally recognized, portfolio-based performance assessment approved by the State Board of Education;

Obtain effective, or comparable, ratings on two, year-end evaluations if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system, State-operated school, or approved nonpublic school approved under COMAR 13A.09.10; or
Complete a comprehensive induction program under § B(2) of this regulation if the individual is employed by a Maryland local school system.
Special Provisions.

The Department shall evaluate credits from institutions in other countries for comparability of degree and coursework by an independent agency authorized to analyze foreign credentials and designated by the Department. The evaluation is final.
Comprehensive Induction Program. A comprehensive induction program shall:
Be developed by a local school system, either independently or collaboratively with other local school systems;
Be approved by the Department;
Last the lesser of 3 years or the amount of time a teacher holds a conditional license; and
Include a locally developed portfolio component that is aligned with the Interstate Teacher Assessment and Support Consortium Standards.

para.text: Delivery of Training Program.
Each election director shall prepare a training program for election judges.
The training program shall include training classes:
Before the primary and general elections in a presidential election year;
Before the primary election in a gubernatorial election year; and
If the State Administrator considers it necessary:
Before the general election in a gubernatorial election year; and
Before a special primary or general election.
The election director shall hold as many training classes and hands-on practice sessions as needed to accommodate all of the election judges.
Requirements for Training Program Completion.
Each election judge shall complete the required training program.
An election director may require an election judge to attend additional classes.
Unless appointed under emergency circumstances, an individual may not serve as an election judge unless the individual completes the required training program.
Requirements for Training Compensation.
A local board shall pay at least $50 to an election judge who completes the training program, and serves as an election judge in the election for which the training was offered.

If the training program includes a training class that is offered through virtual instruction or online delivery and a hands-on practice session, the election judge shall complete the hands-on practice session to be entitled to compensation.

An election judge who is required to complete a training program prior to both a primary and general election is entitled to compensation for each training program, so long as the election judge serves as an election judge in the election for which the training was offered.

para.text: In General. The categories of individuals who may request to have their residence addresses, telephone numbers, and email addresses designated as confidential are as provided in this regulation.
Law Enforcement Personnel. Subject to proof of employment (for example, letter from employer), the following current or former law enforcement personnel may request confidentiality:
Police officers;
Correctional employees with frequent inmate contact;
Members of the State or federal judiciary; and
Prosecutors and investigators employed by prosecutors.
Persons Being Threatened. Subject to proof of a threat to their personal safety, the following individuals may request confidentiality:
Survivors of threatened, attempted, or actual:
Domestic violence;
Sexual assault;
Stalking;
Harassment; or
Human trafficking;
“Stalked” individuals; and
Others whose personal safety has been threatened by unidentified persons.
Victim of or Witness to Felony.

Subject to appropriate documentation satisfactory to the local board, an individual may request confidentiality if the individual is a victim of or a witness to a felony or a delinquent act that, if committed by an adult, would be a felony.
The State may request confidentiality on behalf of a victim of or a witness to a felony or a delinquent act that, if committed by an adult, would be a felony.

Participants in Address Confidentiality Programs. Subject to notification to the State or a local board of participation in the program described in Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, §3-2403, Annotated Code of Maryland, or the program described in State Government Article, §7-303, Annotated Code of Maryland, an individual’s information shall be designated as confidential.

Others. Subject to appropriate documentation satisfactory to the local board, an individual may request confidentiality on the grounds that the disclosure of the individual's residence address, telephone number, or email address:
Poses a threat to the individual's safety; or
Is likely to lead to an unwarranted and serious invasion of privacy.
The following individuals may apply to participate in the Program:
An individual acting on the individual’s own behalf;
A parent or guardian acting on behalf of a minor who resides with the parent or guardian;
A guardian acting on behalf of a disabled person; or
An individual who resides in the same household as an eligible applicant or Program participant.
Form.
A person seeking to keep the person's residence address, telephone number, and email address of a record confidential shall apply in writing on a form approved by the State Administrator.
The form shall be filed:
At the appropriate local board if the applicant wishes to keep the applicant's residence address, telephone number, and email address provided on a voter registration application confidential; or

At the State or local board at which the applicant filed the certificate of candidacy or statement of organization if the applicant wishes to keep the applicant's residence address, telephone number, or email address provided on a certificate of candidacy or statement of organization confidential.
A person seeking confidentiality need not complete the form if:

A person is a participant in the program described in Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, §3-2403, Annotated Code of Maryland, and the Administrative Office of the Courts notifies the State Board; or
A person is a participant in the program described in State Government Article, §7-303, Annotated Code of Maryland, and the Secretary of State notifies the State Board.
Contents. In the application, the applicant shall:
Explain the basis for the request;
Attach any documentation in support of the request, which may include:
Proof of employment;
Certified law enforcement, court, or other federal or State agency records or files;
Documentation from a domestic violence, sexual assault, or human trafficking prevention or assistance program;

Documentation from a religious, medical, or other professional from whom the applicant has sought assistance or treatment as a survivor of threatened, attempted, or actual domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, harassment, or human trafficking; or
A statement from the applicant about the applicant’s subjective fear;
Acknowledge that, notwithstanding approval of the request for confidentiality, the information will be made available:
To the jury commissioner;
To other public officials as required by law; or
Otherwise as may be required by subpoena or other court order;
Waive any right of action against the State, the county, the State Board, the local board, or their employees for failing to keep the information confidential; and
Swear or affirm, under the penalties of perjury, that the information in and accompanying the request is true.
Discretion Required.
Except as provided in §§A(2) and (3) of this regulation, the appropriate board shall determine, in its discretion, whether to grant a request for confidentiality.
The appropriate board shall automatically grant a request made by the State on behalf of a victim or witness under Regulation .02D(2) of this chapter.
Exceptions to Discretion. Board review shall not be required to designate as confidential the identifying information of the following:
Participants in the program described in Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, §3-2403, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Participants in the program described in State Government Article, §7-303 Annotated Code of Maryland.
Verification.
The appropriate board shall:
Consider the explanation provided;
Review the documentation submitted; and
Verify to the extent possible the veracity of the explanation.
This verification may include:
Requiring the applicant to appear in person; and
Contacting an employer, the police, a court clerk, or social service agency.
Any hearing held to consider a request for confidentiality shall be closed to the public.
Confidentiality Pending Decision.
Pending a decision on the application, the appropriate board shall take steps to assure that the records are not revealed to the public.

If a request is made for access to a record for which an application for confidentiality has been made under this chapter, the appropriate board shall complete the application review process before granting or denying the request.
In order to comply with deadlines under the Act, this application shall be:
Given priority over all others pending under this chapter; and
Acted on as promptly as possible.
Periodic Review. The appropriate board shall review all records designated as confidential under this chapter on a regular basis, at least once before each Statewide primary election.

Review on Request for Access. If a request is made for access to a record designated as confidential under this chapter, the appropriate board shall review the confidentiality designation before denying the request.
Scope of Review. In this review, the appropriate board shall:
Verify the confidential home address and telephone number; and
Request confirmation of the continuing need for confidentiality.

The provisions of this regulation shall not apply to designations of confidentiality for participants in the program described in Courts and Judicial Proceedings Article, §3-2403, Annotated Code of Maryland.

para.text: In General. Except as specified in §B of this regulation, electronic communication devices may not be used in a polling place. Prohibited devices include:
Cameras;
Cellular telephones;
Pagers; or
Computer equipment.
Exceptions. This regulation does not apply to:
Cameras used by media representatives as long as the media representatives are recording polling place operations and not a screen or ballot;
Cellular telephones, pagers, or computer equipment used by election officials or law enforcement officers, when acting in their official capacities; or
Cellular telephones or other electronic devices used by a voter:
To provide lawful proof of residency or identification as required by this title;
To review electoral information to assist in marking the voter’s own ballot prior to casting;

To record a digital image of the voter’s own marked ballot prior to casting for personal use if it records only the ballot and may not be used in violation of Election Law Article, §16-206, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
To communicate through a translator or use licensed translation software services as described in Election Law Article, §15.5–103(B), Annotated Code of Maryland.

para.text: Minimum Compensation. For each election judge who has not previously served as an election judge, the compensation for each election day and each early voting day actually served shall be at least $250 per day.

Entitlement to Additional Compensation. For each election judge who has previously served as an election judge, the compensation for each election day and each early voting day shall be at least $100 more per day than the compensation provided to an election judge who has not previously served as an election judge.
Limitations on Additional Compensation. The following individuals are not entitled to additional compensation, unless previously approved by the election director:
Elections judges serving in their first term of office;
Elections judges serving a subsequent term of office, but who only acted as a backup election judge during their first term of office; and
Individuals who perform election related tasks but are not sworn in as an election judge and appointed as an election judge.

Alternative to Compensation. A public high school student who earns hours toward the service-learning requirement for graduation under COMAR 13A.03.02.05 for their time in service as an election judge shall not be compensated for that service under §§A and B of this regulation.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Designated language” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §15.5–101(b), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Licensed translation software services” means a tool or service that provides interpretation of spoken communication between designated languages and English.
“Limited English proficiency” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §15.5–101(c), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Translated” means the process of a trained individual converting from one written language to another while preserving the original meaning.
“Voting materials” has the meaning stated in Election Law Article, §15.5–101(e), Annotated Code of Maryland.

section.heading: Timing for Determination of Significant Need for Language Assistance.
section.text: Not later than 8 months preceding a Statewide primary election, the State Board shall determine whether there is significant need for language assistance in a county.

section.heading: Designating Forms and Instructions Necessary for Voting.

para.text: Timing. Not later than 7 months preceding a Statewide primary election, the State Board may determine if forms and instructions not named in Election Law Article, § 15.5–101(e), Annotated Code of Maryland, shall be designated as necessary to voting.
Procedure. The State Board shall consider recommendations from the State Administrator when designating forms and instructions necessary to voting.

section.heading: Notice of Significant Need for Language Assistance.

para.text: To the Public. Not later than 6 months preceding a Statewide primary election, the State Board shall publish its determinations of significant need for language assistance that includes:
A list of local boards affected; and
The designated languages.

To Local Government Officials. Not later than 6 months preceding a Statewide primary election, the State Administrator shall provide notice of the determination of significant need for language assistance by the State Board to:
The local board of elections; and
The chief financial officer of the jurisdiction.

section.heading: Local Board Requirements.
section.text: Upon determination by the State Board of a significant need for language assistance, the local board shall:

para.text: No later than 11 weeks preceding a Statewide primary election, post translated, downloadable voting materials in designated languages on its website;
Place a State Board approved sign, in each designated language, in each early voting center and polling place;
Ensure the availability in each early voting center or polling place of:
Licensed translation software services; and
A tool or document that communicates in all designated languages and explains how to use the licensed translation software service;
Make a reasonable effort to:
Recruit election judges who can communicate in designated languages;
Assign election judges who speak the designated languages to each early voting center;
Determine which polling places are most likely to serve voters who speak designated languages; and
Assign election judges who speak the designated languages to work in polling places that are most likely to serve voters who speak designated languages; and
Include information about the language assistance program in training materials and programs for election judges and local board of elections staff.

section.heading: Language Assistance in Addition to Required Assistance.

para.text: Form.
The State Board shall:
Prescribe a form for a local board to request approval to provide language assistance services in addition to those required by Regulation .05 of this chapter; and
Provide the form to local boards not later than 1 year preceding a Statewide primary election.
The form shall include questions about:
The population to be served;
The proposed services;
Reasons for the local board’s request; and
Whether approving the request would impose additional costs or duties on the State Board.
A local board seeking to provide language assistance services that exceed requirements shall:
Submit the State Board prescribed form for each additional service or language which it proposes to provide; and
Request State Board approval at least 6 months preceding a Statewide primary election during which the local board proposes to provide additional language assistance services.
Approval of Additional Language Assistance.

Upon receipt of a form requesting approval to provide additional language assistance, the State Administrator shall review and make a recommendation to the State Board to accept or reject the request.
The State Board shall:
Vote on a local board’s proposal within 30 days of receipt of a form requesting approval to provide additional language assistance; and
Within 2 days of its vote on a request to provide additional language assistance, notify the local board of the outcome of the vote.

annotations.annotation: Election Law Article, §§2-102(b)(4), 2-202(b), and 15.5-104, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 2, 2026 (53:4 Md. R. 186)

para.text: In General.
The canvass shall be conducted by the local board, in its role as a board of canvassers, as provided in Election Law Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, and this chapter.

The local board is responsible for enforcing all provisions of Election Law Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, and all rules, regulations, and directives of the State Board that relate to vote canvassing.
The local board is responsible for requesting a waiver from early canvassing of mail-in ballots, as required by Election Law §11-302(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland.
The waiver request shall:
Be in writing to the State Administrator;
Be submitted no later than 17 days prior to the first day of canvass required by Election Law Article, §11-302-(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland; and

Include facts sufficient to establish that the basis for the request is not necessary due to the low number of absentee ballots received by the local board or not practicable due to limited resources or other constraints on the local board.
The State Administrator shall:
Upon receipt of a request for a waiver, the State Administrator shall review the request and determine whether it is sufficient.
Within 5 business days of receiving the request for a waiver, the State Administrator shall notify the local board of the State Administrator’s decision.
Oath or Affirmation.

Each member of the local board shall take and subscribe the following oath or affirmation: I, ........, do swear, (or affirm as the case may be,) that I will support the Constitution of the United States; and that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to the State of Maryland, and support the Constitution and Laws thereof; and that I will, to the best of my skill and judgment, diligently and faithfully, without partiality or prejudice, execute the office of Board of Canvassers, according to the Constitution and Laws of this State.
The oath or affirmation shall be administered and recorded by the clerk of the circuit court.
The oath shall expire 35 days after election day.
Timing of Oath or Affirmation.
Each member of the local board shall take and subscribe the oath or affirmation not later than 9:00 a.m. 8 business days before the first day of early voting; or

If a local board of elections has obtained a waiver under Election Law Article, §11-302(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland, each member of the local board shall take and subscribe the oath or affirmation, not later than 5:00 p.m. on election day.
Constant Presence, Supervision Required.
The canvass of votes at the counting center shall be conducted in the presence and under the direct supervision of the local board.
The board shall be present in the counting center throughout each working session of the canvass.
Oath or Affirmation Required.

Each local board counsel shall take and subscribe the following oath or affirmation: I, ........, do swear, (or affirm as the case may be,) that I will support the Constitution of the United States; and that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to the State of Maryland, and support the Constitution and Laws thereof; and that I will, to the best of my skill and judgment, diligently and faithfully, without partiality or prejudice, serve as counsel to the ........ Board of Canvassers, according to the Constitution and Laws of this State.
The clerk of the circuit court or chairman of the local board shall administer and record the oath or affirmation in §A(1) of this regulation to the local board counsel.
The oath or affirmation shall be administered as follows:
Not later than 9:00 a.m. 8 business days before the first day of early voting; or
If a local board of elections has obtained a waiver under Election Law Article, §11-302(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland, not later than 5:00 p.m. on election day.
The oath shall expire 35 days after election day.
Presence During Decision Sessions.

During each canvass, the local board’s counsel shall be present at any time the local board is deciding questions or otherwise making decisions on the legality or acceptability of a vote on any ballot, an absentee ballot, provisional ballot application, or provisional ballot.
The local board may specifically schedule decision periods, if the schedule is announced at the start of each day of the canvass.
The local board's counsel shall be present throughout all canvass sessions if it appears that absentee or provisional ballots could decide the outcome of a contest or a question.

para.text: United States.
“United States” means the several states, the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, and the Virgin Islands.

“United States” does not include American Samoa, the Canal Zone, Guam, the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands, any other territory or possession of the United States, an Army Post Office address, or a Fleet Post Office address.
In General. An absentee ballot is considered to have been timely received only if:
The ballot is received by the local board office before the polls close on election day;
The ballot is received by a polling place before the polls close at that polling place on election day;
The ballot is deposited into a ballot drop box before the polls close on election day;
The ballot:
Is received by the local board office from the United States Postal Service or a private mail carrier on or before 10 a.m. on the second Friday after an election; and
Was mailed on or before election day, as verified:
By a postmark of the United States Postal Service, an Army Post Office, a Fleet Post Office, or the postal service of any other country; or
By the voter’s affidavit that the ballot was completed and mailed on or before election day, if the return envelope does not contain a postmark or the postmark is illegible; or
The ballot is received by an early voting center before the polls close at that polling place on the last day of early voting.
Untimely Ballots.
A ballot that is not timely received as provided in this regulation shall:
Be retained separately from timely ballots; and
Not be counted.
A ballot that is received after the local board certifies the election may not be:
Presented for rejection to the board of canvassers;
Presented to the local board of elections; or
Counted.

para.text: Election Director Recommendation. The election director shall:
Present the provisional ballot applications to the local board; and
Based on the pre-canvass review of the applications, make recommendations to the local board as to whether the provisional ballot applications should be accepted or rejected.
Votes Required. The local board shall reject a provisional ballot application only by unanimous vote and as provided in this chapter.
Acceptance of Provisional Ballot Application. The local board shall accept a provisional ballot application only if:

The voter is registered to vote, including a voter whose registration status was pending in the precinct register but who satisfied the verification requirement before the start of the provisional canvass;
The voter signed the provisional ballot application;
A voter who was required under COMAR 33.07.06 to provide personal identification information satisfied the identification requirement before the start of the provisional canvass;
In a primary election:
The party affiliation indicated by the voter on the provisional ballot application is the party affiliation of the voter’s record; or
The party affiliation indicated by the voter on the provisional ballot application is not the party affiliation of the voter’s record and the ballot has a nonpartisan Board of Education contest.

The voter voted a provisional ballot because the precinct register indicated that the voter had been issued an absentee ballot or already voted and the voter did not return a voted absentee ballot or voted again in the election;

The voter voted a provisional ballot during extended voting hours and the local board has received confirmation from the State Administrator that the order extending voting hours has not been invalidated;
The voter voted a provisional ballot because the voter’s identity was challenged and the local board, after reviewing the affidavits and any other relevant information, has denied the challenge; and
An individual who was not a pre-qualified voter during early voting or on election day was determined to be eligible to vote and satisfied the proof of residency requirement under COMAR 33.16.04.02E.
Full or Partial Count of Provisional Ballot. If the local board accepts the provisional ballot application, the local board shall count:
The entire provisional ballot if the ballot is the correct ballot style for the voter based on the address and party on the provisional ballot application; or

Only votes for the specific contests for which the voter is entitled to vote based on the address and party on the provisional ballot application, if the ballot is not the correct ballot style for the voter based on the address on the provisional ballot application.

Rejection of Provisional Ballot Application. If the local board does not accept the provisional ballot application as provided in §§B and C of this regulation, the local board shall reject the provisional ballot application.

para.text: Method of Transmission.
Except as provided in §A(2) of this regulation, the local board shall mail by nonforwardable mail a vote-by-mail ballot to each voter.
If a voter submits a timely request for vote-by-mail ballot and requests that the ballot be transmitted by other means, the local board shall transmit the ballot by the method requested by the voter.
Address Where Ballot Mailed.
Except as provided in §B(2) of this regulation, the local board shall mail the vote-by-mail ballot to the voter’s address of record in the Statewide voter registration system.
If the voter submitted a request for a vote-by-mail ballot and provided another address, the local board shall mail the ballot to the address on the request.
Mailing Deadline. A local board shall mail vote-by-mail ballots:
To absent uniformed services voters and overseas voters, at least 45 days before the day of the special election; and
To all other voters, at least 30 days before the day of the special election.
